139	
140	### landing-page-design--pin-objection-answering-microcopy-next-to-the-header-cta — Pin objection-answering microcopy next to the header CTA (_tokens: 408)
141	**Recommendation**: Pair the header's primary CTA with a short risk-reducer line directly adjacent to it, the same way you would under a hero CTA — e.g. "No credit card required" or "Full access" — so the always-visible button answers cost, commitment, or access at the exact moment of click.
142	
143	### landing-page-design--pin-risk-reducer-microcopy-directly-under-the-cta-button — Pin risk-reducer microcopy directly under the CTA button (_tokens: 415)
144	**Recommendation**: Place a short line of risk-reducing microcopy immediately beneath the primary CTA stating the lowest-commitment truth about clicking it: "No credit card required", "Free forever for individuals", "Cancel anytime", or "Takes 30 seconds".
145	
146	### landing-page-design--place-a-logo-bar-near-the-top-but-only-with-brands-the-target-buyer-recognizes — Place a logo bar near the top but only with brands the target buyer recognizes (_tokens: 458)
147	**Recommendation**: Position a customer logo bar high on the page, immediately below or just under the hero, but populate it only with brands the target buyer already recognizes and respects; if you lack recognizable logos, drop the bar rather than fill it with unknown names.
148	
149	### landing-page-design--place-a-quantified-named-customer-proof-point-next-to-the-hero-claim — Place a quantified named-customer proof point next to the hero claim (_tokens: 516)
150	**Recommendation**: Put a specific, quantified social-proof statement immediately adjacent to the hero claim it supports, such as "Used by 8 of the Fortune 50" or "Trusted by 40,000 developers", rather than relying only on a logo strip or a testimonial block at the end of the page.
151	
152	### landing-page-design--place-fit-confirming-social-proof-directly-below-the-pricing-cards — Place fit-confirming social proof directly below the pricing cards (_tokens: 441)
153	**Recommendation**: Put recognizable customer logos or a short testimonial immediately below the pricing cards, before the full feature-comparison table.
154	
155	### landing-page-design--place-the-soc-2-badge-at-the-decision-moment-with-a-verifiable-link-not-buried-in-the-footer — Place the SOC 2 badge at the decision moment with a verifiable link not buried in the footer (_tokens: 579)
156	**Recommendation**: Surface the SOC 2 Type 2 badge where the trust decision happens - directly below or beside the primary CTA and on the pricing page - rather than only in the footer, and make it a verifiable artifact: hyperlink the AICPA logo to www.aicpa.org/soc4so and add one line naming the scope.
157	
158	### landing-page-design--place-the-primary-mobile-cta-in-the-thumb-zone-and-size-every-tap-target-at-least-48px — Place the primary mobile CTA in the thumb zone and size every tap target at least 48px (_tokens: 644)
159	**Recommendation**: On the mobile rendering, keep the primary action within thumb reach (a sticky bottom bar or a CTA in the lower ~40% of the viewport), size every tap target at least 48x48 CSS px, and leave clear space around each one so adjacent controls are not mis-tapped.
160	
161	### landing-page-design--present-self-host-versus-cloud-as-a-price-and-operational-burden-choice-not-a-feature-gate — Present self-host versus cloud as a price and operational-burden choice not a feature gate (_tokens: 577)
162	**Recommendation**: Differentiate the self-hosted open-source option from the managed cloud offering on price and who carries the operational burden, not by withholding core features from self-hosters.
163	
164	### landing-page-design--prove-an-ai-product-with-task-grounded-benchmarks-not-saturated-leaderboard-scores — Prove an AI product with task-grounded benchmarks not saturated leaderboard scores (_tokens: 577)
165	**Recommendation**: In an AI product's benchmark proof section, lead with task-grounded evaluation results — SWE-Bench, agentic tool-call success rate, retrieval faithfulness, or an eval on the buyer's own data — instead of headline scores from saturated generic leaderboards like MMLU.
166	
167	### landing-page-design--prove-ecosystem-fit-with-code-and-language-toggle-tabs-not-just-a-logo-wall — Prove ecosystem fit with code and language-toggle tabs not just a logo wall (_tokens: 618)
168	**Recommendation**: For developer tools, demonstrate that an integration is real by showing the actual connecting code — ideally behind language-toggle tabs (Python/Node/Go) — rather than relying on a logo wall alone.
169	
170	### landing-page-design--prove-the-agent-actually-acts-by-showing-an-end-to-end-task-run-not-a-chat-transcript — Prove the agent actually acts by showing an end-to-end task run not a chat transcript (_tokens: 473)
171	**Recommendation**: Prove genuine agency by showing the agent completing a real multi-step task end to end, with its plan, the tools it called, and the resulting artifact, rather than a chat transcript or a feature list that any rebranded chatbot could claim.
172	
173	### landing-page-design--pull-at-least-one-trust-signal-above-the-mobile-fold-instead-of-letting-proof-sink-down-the-scroll — Pull at least one trust signal above the mobile fold instead of letting proof sink down the scroll (_tokens: 547)
174	**Recommendation**: Ensure at least one concrete trust signal - a quantified proof point, a recognizable customer name, a live GitHub star count, or a security/rating badge - is visible within the first mobile viewport, rather than placing all proof in sections that only appear after a long scroll on a phone.
175	
176	### landing-page-design--put-docs-and-github-with-a-live-star-count-in-the-developer-tool-header — Put Docs and GitHub with a live star count in the developer-tool header (_tokens: 553)
177	**Recommendation**: For a developer tool, give the header a prominent Docs link and a GitHub link, and for an open-source project render the GitHub star count live in the bar. These are not vanity links: for developers, Docs and the repo are primary evaluation paths, and a live star count is persistent social proof shown at every scroll position.
178	
179	### landing-page-design--put-a-copy-paste-quickstart-of-three-steps-or-fewer-near-the-top-of-the-readme — Put a copy-paste quickstart of three steps or fewer near the top of the README (_tokens: 504)
180	**Recommendation**: Give the README a quick-start block high on the page that takes a developer from zero to a working result in roughly three copy-paste commands and under two minutes — install, minimal config, first run — with the exhaustive setup guide deferred to the docs.
181	
182	### landing-page-design--put-real-syntax-highlighted-code-within-the-first-scroll — Put real syntax-highlighted code within the first scroll (_tokens: 514)
183	**Recommendation**: On a developer-tool page, place a real, syntax-highlighted code block (or CLI command + output) high enough that a visitor reaches it within roughly the first 1,000px of scroll — do not bury the first code behind feature copy.
184	
185	### landing-page-design--reduce-the-waitlist-signup-to-an-email-only-single-field — Reduce the waitlist signup to an email-only single field (_tokens: 482)
186	**Recommendation**: Collect only an email address on a waitlist landing page; defer every other field (name, company, role, use case) to a post-signup survey during the nurture sequence.
187	
188	### landing-page-design--repeat-the-primary-cta-down-the-page-and-make-it-sticky-on-scroll — Repeat the primary CTA down the page and make it sticky on scroll (_tokens: 444)
189	**Recommendation**: Carry one consistent primary CTA beyond the hero — repeat it at natural decision points (after the demo, after social proof, after pricing) and keep a sticky version in the nav or a thumb-reach bar so the action is always available as intent peaks.
190	
191	### landing-page-design--replace-coming-soon-with-a-specific-launch-window-or-access-constraint — Replace coming soon with a specific launch window or access constraint (_tokens: 419)
192	**Recommendation**: State a concrete launch window or a real access constraint on the waitlist page instead of an open-ended 'coming soon', which kills the urgency that drives the signup.
193	
194	### landing-page-design--report-multi-run-consistency-or-your-data-eval-not-a-single-best-run-benchmark-number — Report multi-run consistency or your-data eval not a single best-run benchmark number (_tokens: 563)
195	**Recommendation**: In an AI product's benchmark proof section, report results that reflect production reality — consistency across repeated runs, or an eval measured on the buyer's own data — rather than a single best-run lab benchmark number.
196	
197	### landing-page-design--reserve-space-for-hero-media-and-fonts-to-keep-cls-in-the-good-range — Reserve space for hero media and fonts to keep CLS in the Good range (_tokens: 487)
198	**Recommendation**: Reserve the final dimensions of every above-the-fold element — hero image/video, headline, logo bar, CTA — before it loads, so the page does not visibly reflow as assets arrive; keep Cumulative Layout Shift in the Good range (under 0.1).
199	
200	### landing-page-design--scale-use-case-pages-with-programmatic-seo-only-when-each-page-genuinely-serves-its-intent — Scale use-case pages with programmatic SEO only when each page genuinely serves its intent (_tokens: 550)
201	**Recommendation**: Scale a proven use-case page into hundreds of persona/use-case/integration combinations with [[programmatic-seo]], but only ship variants where the template fills in real, differentiated content that serves the long-tail query — not pages that differ by a swapped variable alone.
202	
203	### landing-page-design--ship-a-self-serve-security-or-trust-center-page-that-pre-answers-the-security-questionnaire — Ship a self-serve security or trust-center page that pre-answers the security questionnaire (_tokens: 611)
204	**Recommendation**: Give a B2B dev tool a self-serve security or trust-center page, linked from the footer and the pricing/enterprise tier, that lets the procurement reviewer download the certifications and read the security posture without emailing your team - turning a 200-question back-and-forth into a one-visit confirmation.
205	
206	### landing-page-design--show-only-metrics-that-impress-and-pick-a-different-metric-when-the-obvious-one-is-small — Show only metrics that impress and pick a different metric when the obvious one is small (_tokens: 402)
207	**Recommendation**: Display a usage metric only when its number impresses; when the obvious count (e.g. user count) is small, swap it for a metric that is genuinely large, such as items processed, hours saved, or measured lift.
208	
209	### landing-page-design--show-the-audit-trail-and-reversibility-as-a-first-class-section-for-agentic-products — Show the audit trail and reversibility as a first-class section for agentic products (_tokens: 503)
210	**Recommendation**: Give an agentic product a dedicated governance section that shows the logged action trail, the approval and escalation steps for high-risk actions, and how an action is paused or rolled back, presenting governed execution as a feature rather than fine print.
211	
212	### landing-page-design--show-the-clean-human-handoff-on-agent-failure-as-a-trust-signal — Show the clean human handoff on agent failure as a trust signal (_tokens: 453)
213	**Recommendation**: State on the page what the agent does when it is unsure or fails, showing that it escalates to a human cleanly with full context rather than guessing, instead of implying the agent never errs.
214	
215	### landing-page-design--show-the-human-in-the-loop-boundary-in-the-body-not-just-the-hero-for-agentic-products — Show the human-in-the-loop boundary in the body not just the hero for agentic products (_tokens: 555)
216	**Recommendation**: For agentic AI products, carry the control-boundary message past the hero into the feature/demo body: show concretely what the agent does autonomously, where the human approves or overrides, and what guardrails bound it.
217	
218	### landing-page-design--show-the-tool-working-with-a-demo-gif-or-screenshot-in-the-first-readme-viewport — Show the tool working with a demo GIF or screenshot in the first README viewport (_tokens: 504)
219	**Recommendation**: Place a short demo GIF or a product screenshot in the README's first viewport, right after the one-liner, so a visitor sees the tool working before deciding whether to read on — it is the single highest-leverage above-the-fold element.
220	
221	### landing-page-design--show-three-to-six-core-features-and-cut-the-rest — Show three to six core features and cut the rest (_tokens: 420)
222	**Recommendation**: Limit the features section to the three-to-six capabilities that decide the buyer's fit, and move everything else to the docs; past roughly eight feature blocks the section dilutes and conversion drops.
223	
224	### landing-page-design--state-a-measured-accuracy-figure-and-the-grounding-method-instead-of-claiming-the-ai-is-never-wrong — State a measured accuracy figure and the grounding method instead of claiming the AI is never wrong (_tokens: 553)
225	**Recommendation**: On an AI product page, replace blanket reliability claims with a specific measured accuracy figure plus the mechanism that produces it (retrieval grounding, human-in-the-loop sampling, citations), and acknowledge the residual error rate rather than implying perfection.
226	
227	### landing-page-design--state-the-ai-control-boundary-in-the-hero-for-ai-products — State the AI control boundary in the hero for AI products (_tokens: 494)
228	**Recommendation**: On an AI product hero, state explicitly where the AI acts and where the human stays in control, and bound the claim to realistic outcomes, instead of leaving "AI-powered" abstract.
229	
230	### landing-page-design--state-the-free-tier-usage-limit-in-buyer-language-units-before-signup — State the free-tier usage limit in buyer-language units before signup (_tokens: 525)
231	**Recommendation**: Cap the free or entry tier with a concrete usage limit expressed in the buyer's own units, and show that limit on the pricing page before the visitor signs up.
232	
233	### landing-page-design--strip-the-header-to-a-logo-and-single-cta-on-a-dedicated-landing-page — Strip the header to a logo and single CTA on a dedicated landing page (_tokens: 502)
234	**Recommendation**: On a dedicated landing page — a campaign or ad destination built for one action — remove the navigation entirely and leave only the logo and the single primary CTA. Add light in-page anchor links only for long enterprise pages that need orientation.
235	
236	### landing-page-design--structure-the-faq-as-a-customer-worded-accordion-grouped-by-objection-category-with-faqpage-schema — Structure the FAQ as a customer-worded accordion grouped by objection category with FAQPage schema (_tokens: 595)
237	**Recommendation**: Build the FAQ as an accordion of 10-20 customer-worded questions grouped by objection category, lead each answer with one direct complete sentence, and mark it up with FAQPage JSON-LD schema.
238	
239	### landing-page-design--time-comparison-page-pushes-to-a-competitor-price-hike-outage-or-deprecation — Time comparison-page pushes to a competitor price hike outage or deprecation (_tokens: 496)
240	**Recommendation**: Launch or amplify a "vs competitor" / alternatives page when the named competitor raises prices, removes a free tier, suffers an outage, or deprecates a feature — the moments that convert latent switchers.
241	
242	### landing-page-design--treat-a-prominent-docs-link-as-a-primary-conversion-path-for-developer-tools — Treat a prominent Docs link as a primary conversion path for developer tools (_tokens: 485)
243	**Recommendation**: Give documentation first-class placement — a visible "Docs" link in the header nav and a path to a real quickstart/API reference from the page body — and treat clicks into it as a conversion, not as post-signup support.
244	
245	### landing-page-design--treat-sub-2-second-lcp-as-a-conversion-budget-for-the-landing-page-hero — Treat sub-2-second LCP as a conversion budget for the landing page hero (_tokens: 543)
246	**Recommendation**: Budget the landing page to render its hero (the Largest Contentful Paint element) within 2 seconds, and never let LCP cross the 2.5s "Good" Core Web Vitals threshold — treat load time as a fixed conversion budget the design must fit inside, not a number to optimize after launch.
247	
248	### landing-page-design--turn-the-waitlist-thank-you-screen-into-a-referral-and-queue-position-loop — Turn the waitlist thank-you screen into a referral and queue-position loop (_tokens: 543)
249	**Recommendation**: Treat the post-signup confirmation screen as the highest-intent moment in the funnel: immediately show the new member their queue position and a personal referral link with pre-filled share copy, rewarding referrals with movement up the line.
250	
251	### landing-page-design--use-a-dual-hero-cta-pairing-a-product-action-with-a-docs-or-source-path — Use a dual hero CTA pairing a product action with a docs or source path (_tokens: 477)
252	**Recommendation**: In a developer-tool hero, offer two CTAs: a bold primary button for the commercial/product action and a lighter, visually distinct secondary for immediate self-serve value (docs, GitHub, install command, or playground).
253	
254	### landing-page-design--use-the-homepage-as-a-segmentation-layer-that-routes-each-visitor-to-their-use-case-page — Use the homepage as a segmentation layer that routes each visitor to their use-case page (_tokens: 541)
255	**Recommendation**: For a multi-persona product, let the homepage do segmentation rather than direct conversion — give visitors a fast way to self-identify (a role selector, a Solutions/Use-cases menu, or progressive-disclosure tabs) that routes each to a page with copy, proof, and CTA tuned to them.
256	
257	### landing-page-design--warm-the-waitlist-with-a-staged-email-sequence-and-launch-within-thirty-days — Warm the waitlist with a staged email sequence and launch within thirty days (_tokens: 467)
258	**Recommendation**: Treat a captured waitlist as a perishable asset: run a staged email nurture from the moment of signup and convert the list within 30 days of launch, because an un-nurtured list decays to near-zero conversion after 90 days.
259	
260	### landing-page-design--write-a-disqualifying-subheadline-that-names-the-exact-audience-the-page-is-for — Write a disqualifying subheadline that names the exact audience the page is for (_tokens: 549)
261	**Recommendation**: On a use-case or persona page, make the subheadline name the specific audience or job it serves — 'Built for B2B SaaS teams handling 500+ tickets a month', 'For finance teams that close the books in under five days' — even though it visibly excludes everyone else.
262	
263	### landing-page-design--write-feature-block-headings-as-outcomes-not-feature-names — Write feature-block headings as outcomes not feature names (_tokens: 433)
264	**Recommendation**: Title each feature block with the outcome the developer gets, not the name of the feature — "Improve deliverability", not "SMTP relay"; then let the screenshot and a line of body copy carry the mechanism.
265	
266	### landing-page-design--write-product-specific-cta-copy-not-generic-get-started — Write product-specific CTA copy not generic Get started (_tokens: 339)
267	**Recommendation**: Replace generic CTA labels like "Get started" with copy that names the product's concrete first action: "Start building", "Deploy now", "Download now", "Run it locally".
268	
269	### landing-page-design--write-testimonials-around-a-specific-measurable-outcome-with-full-attribution — Write testimonials around a specific measurable outcome with full attribution (_tokens: 432)
270	**Recommendation**: Lead each testimonial with a specific measurable outcome and attribute it with the person's name, title, and company; cut generic praise like 'Great tool, highly recommend'.
271	
272

file.content:
---
_total: 88
_returned: 88
_offset: 0
_total_tokens: 45372
---

### landing-page-design--add-a-numbered-how-it-works-section-when-the-value-depends-on-non-obvious-mechanics — Add a numbered how-it-works section when the value depends on non-obvious mechanics (_tokens: 544)
**Recommendation**: When the product's value rests on mechanics the static UI does not explain — AI, automation, orchestration — add a short numbered how-it-works section (typically three to four steps) that shows the path from zero to first value, each step paired with an interface screenshot or diagram.

### landing-page-design--answer-developer-objections-in-an-faq-before-they-ask — Answer developer objections in an FAQ before they ask (_tokens: 553)
**Recommendation**: Add a body FAQ that pre-empts the specific objections a developer raises before adopting — stack compatibility, maintenance/abandonment risk, whether they can see and own the code, customizability, and real limits — stated plainly.

### landing-page-design--answer-where-my-data-goes-with-concrete-artifacts-not-a-privacy-label — Answer where my data goes with concrete artifacts not a privacy label (_tokens: 630)
**Recommendation**: Give an AI product page a data-handling block that answers "where does my data go" with concrete, scoped artifacts: an explicit no-training-on-your-data statement, a data-flow or subprocessor summary, retention and deletion specifics, available certifications, and a self-host or zero-retention tier where it exists.

### landing-page-design--build-a-separate-use-case-or-persona-page-for-each-distinct-buyer-instead-of-one-page-for-all — Build a separate use-case or persona page for each distinct buyer instead of one page for all (_tokens: 621)
**Recommendation**: When a developer tool or AI product serves several distinct personas, jobs-to-be-done, or verticals, build one dedicated landing page per segment and tune every element to it, rather than stretching the homepage to cover everyone.

### landing-page-design--build-the-comparison-table-from-specific-metrics-and-admit-your-own-limitations — Build the comparison table from specific metrics and admit your own limitations (_tokens: 578)
**Recommendation**: Build a "vs competitor" comparison table out of specific data points rather than checkmarks, openly mark rows where the competitor is stronger, and include at least one row where you admit your own limitation.

### landing-page-design--build-the-integrations-belt-from-logos-the-buyer-recognizes-and-depends-on — Build the integrations belt from logos the buyer recognizes and depends on (_tokens: 668)
**Recommendation**: Fill the integrations section with logos of the services, platforms, and languages your target buyer already uses and recognizes — chosen for stack-fit signal, not count — and omit aspirational or unrecognized marks.

### landing-page-design--build-the-page-cta-around-team-invites-as-the-paid-conversion-predictor — Build the page CTA around team invites as the paid-conversion predictor (_tokens: 488)
**Recommendation**: For dev tools, design the post-first-success path to surface a team-invite action early, because a team invite is the most reliable predictor of a paid conversion -- a stronger CTA target than pushing the individual toward a paywall.

### landing-page-design--collapse-multi-column-desktop-sections-to-one-column-in-a-deliberate-priority-order-on-mobile — Collapse multi-column desktop sections to one column in a deliberate priority order on mobile (_tokens: 561)
**Recommendation**: When the desktop layout uses multiple columns - a feature grid, a three-tier pricing row, a logo wall, a comparison table - collapse each to a single mobile column in a chosen priority order rather than letting the source order or a naive reflow decide what the visitor sees first.

### landing-page-design--cut-the-sign-up-form-to-three-fields-or-fewer-and-defer-the-rest-to-onboarding — Cut the sign-up form to three fields or fewer and defer the rest to onboarding (_tokens: 521)
**Recommendation**: Ask for the minimum at sign-up — ideally just work email plus password, or a single SSO click — and collect everything else (name, company, role, use case) progressively during onboarding after the account exists.

### landing-page-design--default-to-a-no-credit-card-opt-in-trial-to-maximize-qualified-sign-ups — Default to a no-credit-card opt-in trial to maximize qualified sign-ups (_tokens: 512)
**Recommendation**: For a bottom-up, self-serve developer tool, default the primary CTA to a no-credit-card (opt-in) free trial rather than a card-gated (opt-out) one, accepting a lower trial-to-paid conversion in exchange for more top-of-funnel sign-ups and better-retained users.

### landing-page-design--default-to-annual-billing-and-frame-the-discount-as-absolute-dollars-saved — Default to annual billing and frame the discount as absolute dollars saved (_tokens: 499)
**Recommendation**: Set the annual/monthly toggle to annual by default and express the annual savings as an absolute dollar amount or as free months, not as a bare percentage.

### landing-page-design--defer-non-critical-javascript-so-the-hero-cta-is-interactive-fast — Defer non-critical JavaScript so the hero CTA is interactive fast (_tokens: 504)
**Recommendation**: Defer analytics, chat widgets, A/B-test frameworks, and other non-critical JavaScript so the main thread is free when the hero CTA renders — keep Interaction to Next Paint under the 200ms "Good" threshold so the first click responds instantly.

### landing-page-design--design-the-landing-page-mobile-first-instead-of-retrofitting-the-desktop-layout — Design the landing page mobile-first instead of retrofitting the desktop layout (_tokens: 552)
**Recommendation**: Design and build the landing page for the mobile viewport first, then progressively enhance to desktop, rather than designing a wide desktop layout and squeezing it down with responsive breakpoints.

### landing-page-design--distribute-proof-units-down-the-page-next-to-the-claims-they-support — Distribute proof units down the page next to the claims they support (_tokens: 413)
**Recommendation**: Spread social proof across the page so each unit sits beside the specific claim it backs, rather than collecting all proof into one testimonials block.

### landing-page-design--embed-a-star-history-chart-to-signal-momentum-on-an-active-repo — Embed a star-history chart to signal momentum on an active repo (_tokens: 408)
**Recommendation**: On a repo with a rising star count, embed a star-history chart low in the README as a momentum proof point — it is social proof that the project is alive and growing, which a triaging developer reads as adoption risk reduced.

### landing-page-design--embed-an-ungated-interactive-demo-on-the-product-page-not-a-book-a-demo-gate — Embed an ungated interactive demo on the product page not a Book-a-demo gate (_tokens: 523)
**Recommendation**: Place an interactive, clickable product demo in the page body and leave it ungated — no email wall before the visitor can interact — rather than substituting a "Book a demo" form for the experience.

### landing-page-design--embed-third-party-review-badges-and-security-certs-to-carry-vendor-trust — Embed third-party review badges and security certs to carry vendor trust (_tokens: 436)
**Recommendation**: Surface third-party review badges (G2, Capterra, TrustRadius, Product Hunt) and, for B2B, security/compliance certifications (SOC 2, GDPR) as distinct trust signals separate from your own testimonials.

### landing-page-design--engineer-the-quickstart-for-time-to-first-call-under-five-minutes — Engineer the quickstart for time-to-first-call under five minutes (_tokens: 523)
**Recommendation**: Design the linked quickstart so a developer reaches a first successful API call (a 2xx response) in under five minutes from landing on the docs -- ideally under two to three minutes -- by front-loading a copy-paste-ready call with auth already wired in.

### landing-page-design--frame-ai-product-compatibility-as-works-with-every-mcp-client-from-one-integration — Frame AI-product compatibility as works with every MCP client from one integration (_tokens: 602)
**Recommendation**: For an AI product that exposes an MCP server, lead the compatibility section with the interoperability promise — one integration works with Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Cursor, Copilot, and every other MCP-compatible client — rather than enumerating per-assistant integrations.

### landing-page-design--frame-the-cta-as-a-bounded-trial-not-an-open-ended-sign-up — Frame the CTA as a bounded trial not an open-ended sign-up (_tokens: 462)
**Recommendation**: Word the primary CTA so it reads as a low-commitment, bounded evaluation rather than an open-ended membership: prefer "Trial for free" or "Start free trial" over "Sign up for free" when the entry point is a trial.

### landing-page-design--frame-the-open-source-tier-as-an-unlimited-self-host-option-and-draw-the-paid-line-at-org-level-value — Frame the open-source tier as an unlimited self-host option and draw the paid line at org-level value (_tokens: 769)
**Recommendation**: On an open-core pricing page, present the open-source/self-host option as a genuinely unlimited tier solving the core contributor problem, and gate the paid tiers on organization-level value (governance, SSO, approvals, compliance, support) rather than on crippling the core.

### landing-page-design--highlight-one-recommended-tier-with-a-most-popular-badge-in-the-center — Highlight one recommended tier with a Most Popular badge in the center (_tokens: 434)
**Recommendation**: Visually elevate one middle tier with a "Most Popular" (or "Recommended") badge and center placement, so the page offers an obvious default path through the choice.

### landing-page-design--hold-the-marketing-site-header-to-about-five-links-and-put-a-single-contrasting-cta-top-right — Hold the marketing-site header to about five links and put a single contrasting CTA top-right (_tokens: 527)
**Recommendation**: On a developer-tool or SaaS home/product site, hold the top-level header to roughly five links, place the logo top-left and one contrasting primary CTA top-right, and keep a quieter Login distinct from it. Reserve secondary destinations (support, status, login) for a thin quick-links row or the mega-menu rather than the main bar.

### landing-page-design--instrument-the-docs-as-a-measurable-funnel-with-micro-conversions — Instrument the docs as a measurable funnel with micro-conversions (_tokens: 508)
**Recommendation**: Treat the docs path as a measurable funnel: instrument micro-conversions -- docs-entry, snippet copy, API-key generation, first 2xx call, time-to-first-Explorer-call -- rather than judging the docs by page views or waiting on macro signups.

### landing-page-design--lead-an-agentic-product-page-with-scoped-autonomy-not-full-autonomy — Lead an agentic product page with scoped autonomy not full autonomy (_tokens: 500)
**Recommendation**: Position an agentic product as human-in-the-loop with explicitly scoped autonomy, naming what the agent does unattended and where it stops for approval, rather than leading with a 'fully autonomous' claim.

### landing-page-design--lead-every-feature-block-with-real-product-ui-not-an-abstract-illustration — Lead every feature block with real product UI not an abstract illustration (_tokens: 477)
**Recommendation**: Show each feature with a real product screenshot, code snippet, or short clip of that capability in use — not abstract 3D graphics, stock photos, or decorative illustration.

### landing-page-design--lead-the-hero-headline-with-the-outcome-not-the-feature — Lead the hero headline with the outcome not the feature (_tokens: 468)
**Recommendation**: Write the hero headline to state the visitor's outcome in the fewest words possible, not the product's feature set; a visitor should grasp the value proposition and the next step within about five seconds of load.

### landing-page-design--lead-the-hero-with-one-quantified-claim-as-the-headline — Lead the hero with one quantified claim as the headline (_tokens: 472)
**Recommendation**: When the product has one defensible, quantified claim, make that number the hero headline (e.g. "127x faster", "deploy in 30 seconds") rather than burying it in body copy.

### landing-page-design--lead-the-security-section-with-control-and-data-specifics-developers-verify-not-a-decorative-badge-wall — Lead the security section with control and data specifics developers verify not a decorative badge wall (_tokens: 655)
**Recommendation**: Build the body security section around the concrete specifics a technical reviewer checks - encryption standards, data-handling and retention, self-host or data-residency options, subprocessors - and treat badges as the index into those specifics, not as the whole section.

### landing-page-design--lead-the-waitlist-hero-with-one-named-outcome-under-twelve-words — Lead the waitlist hero with one named outcome under twelve words (_tokens: 477)
**Recommendation**: Headline a waitlist page with a single sentence under twelve words that names one concrete outcome the product delivers, not a feature list and not the fact that it is coming soon.

### landing-page-design--limit-readme-badges-to-three-to-five-functional-trust-signals — Limit README badges to three to five functional trust signals (_tokens: 528)
**Recommendation**: Keep the README badge row to three to five badges that each carry a real trust or freshness signal — license, build/CI status, current package version, last-commit or stars — and drop decorative or vanity badges.

### landing-page-design--make-every-benchmark-claim-auditable-by-linking-the-score-to-its-test-cases-and-pinning-the-version — Make every benchmark claim auditable by linking the score to its test cases and pinning the version (_tokens: 644)
**Recommendation**: In an AI product's benchmark proof section, make each score auditable — link the number to the exact test cases or eval run that produced it, name and version-pin the benchmark, and state the eval methodology — rather than presenting a bare percentage.

### landing-page-design--make-the-features-section-concrete-with-a-what-is-inside-list-not-abstract-benefits — Make the features section concrete with a What-is-Inside list not abstract benefits (_tokens: 491)
**Recommendation**: Replace the abstract benefits section with a concrete "What's Inside" list that names the actual components, versions, and integrations the product ships — the specifics a developer needs to judge fit.

### landing-page-design--match-pricing-transparency-to-deal-size-and-show-a-starting-price-on-the-enterprise-tier — Match pricing transparency to deal size and show a starting price on the enterprise tier (_tokens: 465)
**Recommendation**: Decide how much price to reveal by average contract value: publish full prices when ACV is under ~$25K, show a starting-from price plus a contact option in the ~$25K-100K band, and use a no-price contact-sales tier only for true enterprise.

### landing-page-design--offer-sso-sign-in-and-put-github-first-for-developer-tools — Offer SSO sign-in and put GitHub first for developer tools (_tokens: 455)
**Recommendation**: Offer one-click social sign-in alongside email, and for developer tools lead with GitHub — the auth provider the audience already uses and trusts — then Google, then email as fallback.

### landing-page-design--offer-an-ungated-docs-playground-that-returns-a-real-response-with-no-signup — Offer an ungated docs playground that returns a real response with no signup (_tokens: 528)
**Recommendation**: Embed an interactive API playground in the docs that runs a real request and returns a real response without requiring a signup, so an evaluating developer can reach first success before creating an account.

### landing-page-design--offer-exactly-three-self-serve-tiers-and-separate-enterprise-out — Offer exactly three self-serve tiers and separate enterprise out (_tokens: 464)
**Recommendation**: Present three self-serve pricing tiers (typically free/entry, a mid tier, and a higher tier) and pull any enterprise/contact-sales option out as a visually distinct row or band rather than a fourth priced column.

### landing-page-design--offer-pre-launch-members-an-early-bird-price-or-a-founding-member-value-stack — Offer pre-launch members an early-bird price or a founding-member value stack (_tokens: 473)
**Recommendation**: Reward waitlist members with a concrete early-adopter incentive — a modest early-bird discount or a non-price value stack — surfaced as a reason to join now and to convert at launch.

### landing-page-design--open-a-vs-competitor-page-with-the-verdict-and-one-to-three-differentiators — Open a vs-competitor page with the verdict and one to three differentiators (_tokens: 528)
**Recommendation**: Lead a comparison or alternatives page with a clear verdict in the first 2-3 sentences and anchor it to one to three standout differentiators, not an exhaustive feature list.

### landing-page-design--open-the-readme-with-a-concrete-one-liner-of-ten-words-or-fewer — Open the README with a concrete one-liner of ten words or fewer (_tokens: 455)
**Recommendation**: Make the README's first line a concrete value proposition of ten words or fewer that says what the tool is and why it matters — not a generic category label or a tagline that could describe any project.

### landing-page-design--order-the-readme-above-the-fold-as-one-liner-demo-badges-then-quickstart — Order the README above the fold as one-liner demo badges then quickstart (_tokens: 610)
**Recommendation**: Structure a developer-tool README's first viewport in conversion order — logo plus a one-line value proposition, then a visual demo (GIF or screenshot), then a small row of functional badges, then a copy-paste quick start — and push features, the 'why', docs links, and contributing below that.

### landing-page-design--order-the-devtool-page-body-as-problem-solution-proof-demo-pricing-faq — Order the devtool page body as problem solution proof demo pricing FAQ (_tokens: 629)
**Recommendation**: Sequence the below-hero body of a developer-tool page to mirror the evaluation flow: problem statement, specific solution, a concrete What's-Inside section, a try-it demo/code section, social proof, pricing comparison, an objection FAQ, then a repeated CTA.

### landing-page-design--pair-the-comparison-page-with-a-phased-migration-guide-and-a-switching-incentive — Pair the comparison page with a phased migration guide and a switching incentive (_tokens: 534)
**Recommendation**: Attach to the comparison page a concrete switching path — a phased migration guide with before/after code, an automated or one-click import, and a time-bound migration credit — so the confirmed decision can be acted on in place.

### landing-page-design--pick-the-feature-section-layout-from-the-feature-type — Pick the feature-section layout from the feature type (_tokens: 492)
**Recommendation**: Choose the feature-section layout to fit the features, not the trend: alternating left-right rows for a few flagship features that deserve a narrative, a bento grid when several features are roughly equal, full-width scrollable belts for many small elements, tabs to group features by persona, and a step-by-step layout for anything that is really a process.

### landing-page-design--pin-objection-answering-microcopy-next-to-the-header-cta — Pin objection-answering microcopy next to the header CTA (_tokens: 408)
**Recommendation**: Pair the header's primary CTA with a short risk-reducer line directly adjacent to it, the same way you would under a hero CTA — e.g. "No credit card required" or "Full access" — so the always-visible button answers cost, commitment, or access at the exact moment of click.

### landing-page-design--pin-risk-reducer-microcopy-directly-under-the-cta-button — Pin risk-reducer microcopy directly under the CTA button (_tokens: 415)
**Recommendation**: Place a short line of risk-reducing microcopy immediately beneath the primary CTA stating the lowest-commitment truth about clicking it: "No credit card required", "Free forever for individuals", "Cancel anytime", or "Takes 30 seconds".

### landing-page-design--place-a-logo-bar-near-the-top-but-only-with-brands-the-target-buyer-recognizes — Place a logo bar near the top but only with brands the target buyer recognizes (_tokens: 458)
**Recommendation**: Position a customer logo bar high on the page, immediately below or just under the hero, but populate it only with brands the target buyer already recognizes and respects; if you lack recognizable logos, drop the bar rather than fill it with unknown names.

### landing-page-design--place-a-quantified-named-customer-proof-point-next-to-the-hero-claim — Place a quantified named-customer proof point next to the hero claim (_tokens: 516)
**Recommendation**: Put a specific, quantified social-proof statement immediately adjacent to the hero claim it supports, such as "Used by 8 of the Fortune 50" or "Trusted by 40,000 developers", rather than relying only on a logo strip or a testimonial block at the end of the page.

### landing-page-design--place-fit-confirming-social-proof-directly-below-the-pricing-cards — Place fit-confirming social proof directly below the pricing cards (_tokens: 441)
**Recommendation**: Put recognizable customer logos or a short testimonial immediately below the pricing cards, before the full feature-comparison table.

### landing-page-design--place-the-soc-2-badge-at-the-decision-moment-with-a-verifiable-link-not-buried-in-the-footer — Place the SOC 2 badge at the decision moment with a verifiable link not buried in the footer (_tokens: 579)
**Recommendation**: Surface the SOC 2 Type 2 badge where the trust decision happens - directly below or beside the primary CTA and on the pricing page - rather than only in the footer, and make it a verifiable artifact: hyperlink the AICPA logo to www.aicpa.org/soc4so and add one line naming the scope.

### landing-page-design--place-the-primary-mobile-cta-in-the-thumb-zone-and-size-every-tap-target-at-least-48px — Place the primary mobile CTA in the thumb zone and size every tap target at least 48px (_tokens: 644)
**Recommendation**: On the mobile rendering, keep the primary action within thumb reach (a sticky bottom bar or a CTA in the lower ~40% of the viewport), size every tap target at least 48x48 CSS px, and leave clear space around each one so adjacent controls are not mis-tapped.

### landing-page-design--present-self-host-versus-cloud-as-a-price-and-operational-burden-choice-not-a-feature-gate — Present self-host versus cloud as a price and operational-burden choice not a feature gate (_tokens: 577)
**Recommendation**: Differentiate the self-hosted open-source option from the managed cloud offering on price and who carries the operational burden, not by withholding core features from self-hosters.

### landing-page-design--prove-an-ai-product-with-task-grounded-benchmarks-not-saturated-leaderboard-scores — Prove an AI product with task-grounded benchmarks not saturated leaderboard scores (_tokens: 577)
**Recommendation**: In an AI product's benchmark proof section, lead with task-grounded evaluation results — SWE-Bench, agentic tool-call success rate, retrieval faithfulness, or an eval on the buyer's own data — instead of headline scores from saturated generic leaderboards like MMLU.

### landing-page-design--prove-ecosystem-fit-with-code-and-language-toggle-tabs-not-just-a-logo-wall — Prove ecosystem fit with code and language-toggle tabs not just a logo wall (_tokens: 618)
**Recommendation**: For developer tools, demonstrate that an integration is real by showing the actual connecting code — ideally behind language-toggle tabs (Python/Node/Go) — rather than relying on a logo wall alone.

### landing-page-design--prove-the-agent-actually-acts-by-showing-an-end-to-end-task-run-not-a-chat-transcript — Prove the agent actually acts by showing an end-to-end task run not a chat transcript (_tokens: 473)
**Recommendation**: Prove genuine agency by showing the agent completing a real multi-step task end to end, with its plan, the tools it called, and the resulting artifact, rather than a chat transcript or a feature list that any rebranded chatbot could claim.

### landing-page-design--pull-at-least-one-trust-signal-above-the-mobile-fold-instead-of-letting-proof-sink-down-the-scroll — Pull at least one trust signal above the mobile fold instead of letting proof sink down the scroll (_tokens: 547)
**Recommendation**: Ensure at least one concrete trust signal - a quantified proof point, a recognizable customer name, a live GitHub star count, or a security/rating badge - is visible within the first mobile viewport, rather than placing all proof in sections that only appear after a long scroll on a phone.

### landing-page-design--put-docs-and-github-with-a-live-star-count-in-the-developer-tool-header — Put Docs and GitHub with a live star count in the developer-tool header (_tokens: 553)
**Recommendation**: For a developer tool, give the header a prominent Docs link and a GitHub link, and for an open-source project render the GitHub star count live in the bar. These are not vanity links: for developers, Docs and the repo are primary evaluation paths, and a live star count is persistent social proof shown at every scroll position.

### landing-page-design--put-a-copy-paste-quickstart-of-three-steps-or-fewer-near-the-top-of-the-readme — Put a copy-paste quickstart of three steps or fewer near the top of the README (_tokens: 504)
**Recommendation**: Give the README a quick-start block high on the page that takes a developer from zero to a working result in roughly three copy-paste commands and under two minutes — install, minimal config, first run — with the exhaustive setup guide deferred to the docs.

### landing-page-design--put-real-syntax-highlighted-code-within-the-first-scroll — Put real syntax-highlighted code within the first scroll (_tokens: 514)
**Recommendation**: On a developer-tool page, place a real, syntax-highlighted code block (or CLI command + output) high enough that a visitor reaches it within roughly the first 1,000px of scroll — do not bury the first code behind feature copy.

### landing-page-design--reduce-the-waitlist-signup-to-an-email-only-single-field — Reduce the waitlist signup to an email-only single field (_tokens: 482)
**Recommendation**: Collect only an email address on a waitlist landing page; defer every other field (name, company, role, use case) to a post-signup survey during the nurture sequence.

### landing-page-design--repeat-the-primary-cta-down-the-page-and-make-it-sticky-on-scroll — Repeat the primary CTA down the page and make it sticky on scroll (_tokens: 444)
**Recommendation**: Carry one consistent primary CTA beyond the hero — repeat it at natural decision points (after the demo, after social proof, after pricing) and keep a sticky version in the nav or a thumb-reach bar so the action is always available as intent peaks.

### landing-page-design--replace-coming-soon-with-a-specific-launch-window-or-access-constraint — Replace coming soon with a specific launch window or access constraint (_tokens: 419)
**Recommendation**: State a concrete launch window or a real access constraint on the waitlist page instead of an open-ended 'coming soon', which kills the urgency that drives the signup.

### landing-page-design--report-multi-run-consistency-or-your-data-eval-not-a-single-best-run-benchmark-number — Report multi-run consistency or your-data eval not a single best-run benchmark number (_tokens: 563)
**Recommendation**: In an AI product's benchmark proof section, report results that reflect production reality — consistency across repeated runs, or an eval measured on the buyer's own data — rather than a single best-run lab benchmark number.

### landing-page-design--reserve-space-for-hero-media-and-fonts-to-keep-cls-in-the-good-range — Reserve space for hero media and fonts to keep CLS in the Good range (_tokens: 487)
**Recommendation**: Reserve the final dimensions of every above-the-fold element — hero image/video, headline, logo bar, CTA — before it loads, so the page does not visibly reflow as assets arrive; keep Cumulative Layout Shift in the Good range (under 0.1).

### landing-page-design--scale-use-case-pages-with-programmatic-seo-only-when-each-page-genuinely-serves-its-intent — Scale use-case pages with programmatic SEO only when each page genuinely serves its intent (_tokens: 550)
**Recommendation**: Scale a proven use-case page into hundreds of persona/use-case/integration combinations with [[programmatic-seo]], but only ship variants where the template fills in real, differentiated content that serves the long-tail query — not pages that differ by a swapped variable alone.

### landing-page-design--ship-a-self-serve-security-or-trust-center-page-that-pre-answers-the-security-questionnaire — Ship a self-serve security or trust-center page that pre-answers the security questionnaire (_tokens: 611)
**Recommendation**: Give a B2B dev tool a self-serve security or trust-center page, linked from the footer and the pricing/enterprise tier, that lets the procurement reviewer download the certifications and read the security posture without emailing your team - turning a 200-question back-and-forth into a one-visit confirmation.

### landing-page-design--show-only-metrics-that-impress-and-pick-a-different-metric-when-the-obvious-one-is-small — Show only metrics that impress and pick a different metric when the obvious one is small (_tokens: 402)
**Recommendation**: Display a usage metric only when its number impresses; when the obvious count (e.g. user count) is small, swap it for a metric that is genuinely large, such as items processed, hours saved, or measured lift.

### landing-page-design--show-the-audit-trail-and-reversibility-as-a-first-class-section-for-agentic-products — Show the audit trail and reversibility as a first-class section for agentic products (_tokens: 503)
**Recommendation**: Give an agentic product a dedicated governance section that shows the logged action trail, the approval and escalation steps for high-risk actions, and how an action is paused or rolled back, presenting governed execution as a feature rather than fine print.

### landing-page-design--show-the-clean-human-handoff-on-agent-failure-as-a-trust-signal — Show the clean human handoff on agent failure as a trust signal (_tokens: 453)
**Recommendation**: State on the page what the agent does when it is unsure or fails, showing that it escalates to a human cleanly with full context rather than guessing, instead of implying the agent never errs.

### landing-page-design--show-the-human-in-the-loop-boundary-in-the-body-not-just-the-hero-for-agentic-products — Show the human-in-the-loop boundary in the body not just the hero for agentic products (_tokens: 555)
**Recommendation**: For agentic AI products, carry the control-boundary message past the hero into the feature/demo body: show concretely what the agent does autonomously, where the human approves or overrides, and what guardrails bound it.

### landing-page-design--show-the-tool-working-with-a-demo-gif-or-screenshot-in-the-first-readme-viewport — Show the tool working with a demo GIF or screenshot in the first README viewport (_tokens: 504)
**Recommendation**: Place a short demo GIF or a product screenshot in the README's first viewport, right after the one-liner, so a visitor sees the tool working before deciding whether to read on — it is the single highest-leverage above-the-fold element.

### landing-page-design--show-three-to-six-core-features-and-cut-the-rest — Show three to six core features and cut the rest (_tokens: 420)
**Recommendation**: Limit the features section to the three-to-six capabilities that decide the buyer's fit, and move everything else to the docs; past roughly eight feature blocks the section dilutes and conversion drops.

### landing-page-design--state-a-measured-accuracy-figure-and-the-grounding-method-instead-of-claiming-the-ai-is-never-wrong — State a measured accuracy figure and the grounding method instead of claiming the AI is never wrong (_tokens: 553)
**Recommendation**: On an AI product page, replace blanket reliability claims with a specific measured accuracy figure plus the mechanism that produces it (retrieval grounding, human-in-the-loop sampling, citations), and acknowledge the residual error rate rather than implying perfection.

### landing-page-design--state-the-ai-control-boundary-in-the-hero-for-ai-products — State the AI control boundary in the hero for AI products (_tokens: 494)
**Recommendation**: On an AI product hero, state explicitly where the AI acts and where the human stays in control, and bound the claim to realistic outcomes, instead of leaving "AI-powered" abstract.

### landing-page-design--state-the-free-tier-usage-limit-in-buyer-language-units-before-signup — State the free-tier usage limit in buyer-language units before signup (_tokens: 525)
**Recommendation**: Cap the free or entry tier with a concrete usage limit expressed in the buyer's own units, and show that limit on the pricing page before the visitor signs up.

### landing-page-design--strip-the-header-to-a-logo-and-single-cta-on-a-dedicated-landing-page — Strip the header to a logo and single CTA on a dedicated landing page (_tokens: 502)
**Recommendation**: On a dedicated landing page — a campaign or ad destination built for one action — remove the navigation entirely and leave only the logo and the single primary CTA. Add light in-page anchor links only for long enterprise pages that need orientation.

### landing-page-design--structure-the-faq-as-a-customer-worded-accordion-grouped-by-objection-category-with-faqpage-schema — Structure the FAQ as a customer-worded accordion grouped by objection category with FAQPage schema (_tokens: 595)
**Recommendation**: Build the FAQ as an accordion of 10-20 customer-worded questions grouped by objection category, lead each answer with one direct complete sentence, and mark it up with FAQPage JSON-LD schema.

### landing-page-design--time-comparison-page-pushes-to-a-competitor-price-hike-outage-or-deprecation — Time comparison-page pushes to a competitor price hike outage or deprecation (_tokens: 496)
**Recommendation**: Launch or amplify a "vs competitor" / alternatives page when the named competitor raises prices, removes a free tier, suffers an outage, or deprecates a feature — the moments that convert latent switchers.

### landing-page-design--treat-a-prominent-docs-link-as-a-primary-conversion-path-for-developer-tools — Treat a prominent Docs link as a primary conversion path for developer tools (_tokens: 485)
**Recommendation**: Give documentation first-class placement — a visible "Docs" link in the header nav and a path to a real quickstart/API reference from the page body — and treat clicks into it as a conversion, not as post-signup support.

### landing-page-design--treat-sub-2-second-lcp-as-a-conversion-budget-for-the-landing-page-hero — Treat sub-2-second LCP as a conversion budget for the landing page hero (_tokens: 543)
**Recommendation**: Budget the landing page to render its hero (the Largest Contentful Paint element) within 2 seconds, and never let LCP cross the 2.5s "Good" Core Web Vitals threshold — treat load time as a fixed conversion budget the design must fit inside, not a number to optimize after launch.

### landing-page-design--turn-the-waitlist-thank-you-screen-into-a-referral-and-queue-position-loop — Turn the waitlist thank-you screen into a referral and queue-position loop (_tokens: 543)
**Recommendation**: Treat the post-signup confirmation screen as the highest-intent moment in the funnel: immediately show the new member their queue position and a personal referral link with pre-filled share copy, rewarding referrals with movement up the line.

### landing-page-design--use-a-dual-hero-cta-pairing-a-product-action-with-a-docs-or-source-path — Use a dual hero CTA pairing a product action with a docs or source path (_tokens: 477)
**Recommendation**: In a developer-tool hero, offer two CTAs: a bold primary button for the commercial/product action and a lighter, visually distinct secondary for immediate self-serve value (docs, GitHub, install command, or playground).

### landing-page-design--use-the-homepage-as-a-segmentation-layer-that-routes-each-visitor-to-their-use-case-page — Use the homepage as a segmentation layer that routes each visitor to their use-case page (_tokens: 541)
**Recommendation**: For a multi-persona product, let the homepage do segmentation rather than direct conversion — give visitors a fast way to self-identify (a role selector, a Solutions/Use-cases menu, or progressive-disclosure tabs) that routes each to a page with copy, proof, and CTA tuned to them.

### landing-page-design--warm-the-waitlist-with-a-staged-email-sequence-and-launch-within-thirty-days — Warm the waitlist with a staged email sequence and launch within thirty days (_tokens: 467)
**Recommendation**: Treat a captured waitlist as a perishable asset: run a staged email nurture from the moment of signup and convert the list within 30 days of launch, because an un-nurtured list decays to near-zero conversion after 90 days.

### landing-page-design--write-a-disqualifying-subheadline-that-names-the-exact-audience-the-page-is-for — Write a disqualifying subheadline that names the exact audience the page is for (_tokens: 549)
**Recommendation**: On a use-case or persona page, make the subheadline name the specific audience or job it serves — 'Built for B2B SaaS teams handling 500+ tickets a month', 'For finance teams that close the books in under five days' — even though it visibly excludes everyone else.

### landing-page-design--write-feature-block-headings-as-outcomes-not-feature-names — Write feature-block headings as outcomes not feature names (_tokens: 433)
**Recommendation**: Title each feature block with the outcome the developer gets, not the name of the feature — "Improve deliverability", not "SMTP relay"; then let the screenshot and a line of body copy carry the mechanism.

### landing-page-design--write-product-specific-cta-copy-not-generic-get-started — Write product-specific CTA copy not generic Get started (_tokens: 339)
**Recommendation**: Replace generic CTA labels like "Get started" with copy that names the product's concrete first action: "Start building", "Deploy now", "Download now", "Run it locally".

### landing-page-design--write-testimonials-around-a-specific-measurable-outcome-with-full-attribution — Write testimonials around a specific measurable outcome with full attribution (_tokens: 432)
**Recommendation**: Lead each testimonial with a specific measurable outcome and attribute it with the person's name, title, and company; cut generic praise like 'Great tool, highly recommend'.

content.text: Now the pitfalls, caveats, and sources lists.

input.description: List all pitfalls in the mem

input.command: cd ~/Projekte/expertise && memstead list --mem landing-page-design --type caveat --quiet --limit 20 2>&1 && echo ===SOURCES=== && memstead list --mem landing-page-design --type source --quiet --limit 70 2>&1 | head -200
input.description: List caveats and sources in the mem

content.content:
---
_total: 46
_returned: 46
_offset: 0
_total_tokens: 16658
---

### landing-page-design--asking-for-company-role-and-use-case-before-the-account-exists — Asking for company role and use case before the account exists (_tokens: 331)
**Mistake**: Front-loading the sign-up form with qualification and personalization fields — role, team size, use case, how-did-you-hear — that marketing and sales want, before the visitor has created an account.

### landing-page-design--asking-for-name-company-and-use-case-on-the-waitlist-form — Asking for name company and use case on the waitlist form (_tokens: 259)
**Mistake**: Adding name, company, role, or use-case fields to a waitlist signup form to collect richer data up front.

### landing-page-design--building-persona-pages-before-the-icp-and-core-message-are-defined — Building persona pages before the ICP and core message are defined (_tokens: 372)
**Mistake**: Spinning up a grid of persona and use-case pages before the product has a defined ICP, validated positioning, and a core message that converts on a single page.

### landing-page-design--calling-a-product-an-ai-agent-while-the-page-only-shows-a-chat-box — Calling a product an AI agent while the page only shows a chat box (_tokens: 285)
**Mistake**: Branding the product as an 'AI agent' while the page only demonstrates a chat box or a content-generation widget that never takes a real action.

### landing-page-design--claiming-the-ai-is-fully-autonomous-and-never-makes-mistakes — Claiming the AI is fully autonomous and never makes mistakes (_tokens: 340)
**Mistake**: Positioning an AI product with unbounded superlatives "fully autonomous", "100% accurate", "never makes mistakes" with no measured figure, grounding mechanism, or human-control story.

### landing-page-design--clawing-back-previously-open-features-behind-the-paywall — Clawing back previously-open features behind the paywall (_tokens: 421)
**Mistake**: Moving a feature that shipped as open source into a proprietary paid tier - or relicensing the core away from OSI-open - and reflecting that on the pricing page as a newly gated capability.

### landing-page-design--cluttering-the-readme-with-a-wall-of-decorative-and-vanity-badges — Cluttering the README with a wall of decorative and vanity badges (_tokens: 425)
**Mistake**: Stacking eight or more badges across the top of the README — mixing functional ones with decorative marks ('Made with love', 'Awesome', 'PRs welcome') and vanity metrics (download counts, coverage percentages) — in the belief that more badges signal a more serious project.

### landing-page-design--crowding-the-hero-with-multiple-competing-ctas — Crowding the hero with multiple competing CTAs (_tokens: 289)
**Mistake**: Placing several equally-weighted calls-to-action in the hero (sign up, book a demo, watch video, read pricing, contact sales) so none is clearly the primary.

### landing-page-design--decorating-the-features-section-with-abstract-3d-graphics-instead-of-the-product — Decorating the features section with abstract 3D graphics instead of the product (_tokens: 343)
**Mistake**: Illustrating each feature with abstract 3D renders, gradient blobs, mascots, or stock photography instead of a screenshot, code snippet, or clip of the actual product.

### landing-page-design--delaying-the-waitlist-confirmation-so-the-referral-share-window-closes — Delaying the waitlist confirmation so the referral share window closes (_tokens: 303)
**Mistake**: Showing a bare 'thanks, we'll be in touch' confirmation and sending the referral link by email hours later, instead of surfacing the share mechanic instantly.

### landing-page-design--dumping-decorative-or-expired-security-badges-in-the-footer-instead-of-placing-one-verifiable-badge-at-the-decision-point — Dumping decorative or expired security badges in the footer instead of placing one verifiable badge at the decision point (_tokens: 478)
**Mistake**: Treating security badges as footer decoration - dropping a row of SOC 2 / ISO / 'secure' logos at the bottom of the page with no link, no scope, sometimes expired, and far from the CTA where the trust decision is actually made.

### landing-page-design--dumping-the-full-changelog-architecture-and-api-reference-into-the-readme — Dumping the full changelog architecture and API reference into the README (_tokens: 461)
**Mistake**: Treating the README as the project's complete documentation — pasting the full changelog, architecture deep-dive, exhaustive configuration, and API reference into one long file — instead of keeping it to the landing-page job and linking out to the docs.

### landing-page-design--filling-the-comparison-table-with-checkmarks-instead-of-data — Filling the comparison table with checkmarks instead of data (_tokens: 370)
**Mistake**: Building the "vs competitor" table out of generic ✓/✗ cells ("Integrations ✓ vs ✓", "Fast ✓ vs ✗") instead of specific values.

### landing-page-design--filling-the-converting-page-header-with-a-full-sitemap-of-links — Filling the converting-page header with a full sitemap of links (_tokens: 337)
**Mistake**: Treating the header of a converting page like a sitemap — packing in every section (Product, Solutions, Use cases, Resources, Blog, Company, Partners, Support, Status, Login, Sign up) so the bar mirrors the whole site.

### landing-page-design--forcing-a-wide-comparison-table-or-feature-grid-to-horizontal-scroll-on-mobile — Forcing a wide comparison table or feature grid to horizontal-scroll on mobile (_tokens: 437)
**Mistake**: Keeping a multi-column comparison table, pricing grid, or feature matrix at its desktop width on mobile, so the visitor must scroll sideways - or pinch-zoom - to read columns that run off the edge of the screen.

### landing-page-design--gating-the-interactive-demo-behind-an-email-form — Gating the interactive demo behind an email form (_tokens: 365)
**Mistake**: Putting the interactive product demo behind an email/lead-capture wall, or replacing the demo entirely with a "Book a demo" form, so the visitor cannot try anything without handing over contact details first.

### landing-page-design--gating-the-quickstart-behind-signup-so-first-success-comes-after-the-account — Gating the quickstart behind signup so first success comes after the account (_tokens: 376)
**Mistake**: Requiring an account, an approved API key, or a sales conversation before a developer can run the first call -- so the quickstart only works after signup and time-to-first-success is measured in hours, not minutes.

### landing-page-design--giving-the-header-login-the-same-visual-weight-as-the-primary-cta — Giving the header Login the same visual weight as the primary CTA (_tokens: 314)
**Mistake**: Rendering the header's Login and the primary acquisition CTA as two identically-styled buttons side by side, so a new visitor sees two equally-weighted choices.

### landing-page-design--headlining-a-saturated-generic-leaderboard-score-where-every-model-clusters — Headlining a saturated generic leaderboard score where every model clusters (_tokens: 393)
**Mistake**: Leading an AI product's proof with a headline score on a saturated generic leaderboard — e.g. an MMLU percentage — where every frontier model already scores in the same high band.

### landing-page-design--hiding-all-prices-behind-contact-sales-on-a-self-serve-product — Hiding all prices behind Contact sales on a self-serve product (_tokens: 321)
**Mistake**: Replacing every tier's price with a "Contact sales" or "Request a quote" button on a product that is sold self-serve to individual developers or small teams.

### landing-page-design--hiding-the-entire-security-posture-behind-contact-sales-instead-of-a-self-serve-security-page — Hiding the entire security posture behind Contact sales instead of a self-serve security page (_tokens: 452)
**Mistake**: Forcing every security question through a sales rep - no public security page, certifications available 'on request', the SOC 2 report only after a call - so the procurement reviewer cannot confirm anything without entering the sales funnel.

### landing-page-design--jumping-straight-to-installation-without-saying-what-the-tool-is-or-why-it-exists — Jumping straight to installation without saying what the tool is or why it exists (_tokens: 439)
**Mistake**: Opening the README with the installation command — skipping the one-liner, the demo, and the 'why this exists' — so the first thing a visitor sees is how to install a thing they have not yet been told the purpose of.

### landing-page-design--leading-with-fully-autonomous-as-the-headline-claim-for-an-agentic-product — Leading with fully autonomous as the headline claim for an agentic product (_tokens: 297)
**Mistake**: Headlining an agentic product with 'fully autonomous' or 'works without you' as the lead value claim.

### landing-page-design--letting-the-waitlist-go-silent-between-signup-and-launch — Letting the waitlist go silent between signup and launch (_tokens: 283)
**Mistake**: Capturing waitlist emails and then sending nothing until launch day, treating the list as a static asset that will convert on its own.

### landing-page-design--low-contrast-integration-logos-that-disappear-against-the-section-background — Low-contrast integration logos that disappear against the section background (_tokens: 316)
**Mistake**: Rendering integration logos too faint against the section background — typically monochrome or low-opacity marks on a dark 'connect your stack' band — so the visitor cannot read which tools are supported.

### landing-page-design--measuring-landing-page-speed-only-on-a-fast-desktop-on-office-wifi — Measuring landing page speed only on a fast desktop on office wifi (_tokens: 404)
**Mistake**: Judging the page "fast enough" because it loads instantly on the developer's high-end laptop on office wifi, instead of measuring real-user field data on mid-range mobile devices and cellular networks.

### landing-page-design--offering-five-or-more-tiers-or-stuffing-every-feature-into-each-card — Offering five or more tiers or stuffing every feature into each card (_tokens: 343)
**Mistake**: Presenting five or more pricing tiers, and/or listing every feature on each tier card so the cards become long undifferentiated checklists.

### landing-page-design--padding-the-faq-with-marketing-softballs-instead-of-real-objections — Padding the FAQ with marketing softballs instead of real objections (_tokens: 452)
**Mistake**: Filling the FAQ with planted promotional questions — "Why are you the best?", "What makes you different?", "Why should I choose you over alternatives?" — instead of the real risk questions a developer would actually ask before adopting.

### landing-page-design--padding-the-integrations-belt-with-aspirational-or-unrecognized-logos — Padding the integrations belt with aspirational or unrecognized logos (_tokens: 334)
**Mistake**: Filling the integrations section with as many logos as possible — including marks the buyer does not recognize, or integrations that are planned or trivial rather than real and depended-upon.

### landing-page-design--presenting-a-cherry-picked-or-unauditable-benchmark-number-as-marketing-math — Presenting a cherry-picked or unauditable benchmark number as marketing math (_tokens: 499)
**Mistake**: Putting a single bare benchmark percentage on the page — unversioned, single-best-run, no link to the test cases, no methodology — chosen because it flatters the product.

### landing-page-design--presenting-features-as-a-flat-function-list-with-no-problem-or-outcome — Presenting features as a flat function list with no problem or outcome (_tokens: 360)
**Mistake**: Listing capabilities as a flat grid of feature-named tiles ("SSO", "Webhooks", "Audit log") with no outcome framing and no connection to a user problem — the bare function list.

### landing-page-design--printing-a-small-vanity-user-count-that-signals-failure — Printing a small vanity user count that signals failure (_tokens: 254)
**Mistake**: Displaying the obvious usage metric (e.g. 'Join 12 users') when that number is small, on the assumption that any number is reassuring.

### landing-page-design--reassuring-ai-buyers-with-a-bare-private-and-secure-badge-instead-of-data-specifics — Reassuring AI buyers with a bare private and secure badge instead of data specifics (_tokens: 353)
**Mistake**: Addressing AI data-privacy anxiety with brand-level reassurance words alone "private", "secure", "enterprise-grade", or a lone SOC 2 badge without saying what happens to the buyer's data.

### landing-page-design--reassuring-agent-buyers-with-a-governed-badge-instead-of-showing-the-audit-trail — Reassuring agent buyers with a governed badge instead of showing the audit trail (_tokens: 303)
**Mistake**: Answering the control objection with a vague 'governed' or 'enterprise-grade oversight' badge instead of showing the concrete audit log, approval steps, and rollback path.

### landing-page-design--requiring-a-credit-card-on-a-self-serve-product-to-start-a-trial — Requiring a credit card on a self-serve product to start a trial (_tokens: 327)
**Mistake**: Gating a self-serve, bottom-up product's free trial behind a credit-card form, so a developer must enter payment details before trying anything.

### landing-page-design--shipping-a-coming-soon-page-with-no-clear-single-action — Shipping a coming-soon page with no clear single action (_tokens: 281)
**Mistake**: Putting up a 'coming soon' splash that announces the product is on its way but gives the visitor no specific action, no outcome, and no reason to commit now.

### landing-page-design--shipping-a-desktop-first-landing-page-that-only-gets-squeezed-down-for-mobile — Shipping a desktop-first landing page that only gets squeezed down for mobile (_tokens: 464)
**Mistake**: Designing the landing page for a wide desktop canvas and treating mobile as an afterthought handled by responsive breakpoints - a hero that overflows or reorders awkwardly, multi-column grids that reflow into a confusing order, and a CTA stranded out of thumb reach on the phone.

### landing-page-design--shipping-thin-programmatic-use-case-pages-that-only-swap-a-variable — Shipping thin programmatic use-case pages that only swap a variable (_tokens: 351)
**Mistake**: Generating hundreds of use-case or persona pages from a template where the only thing that changes per page is a substituted noun — the same body, the same proof, the same screenshots, with '[persona]' or '[integration]' swapped in the headline.

### landing-page-design--shipping-unedited-ai-generated-hero-copy-with-detectable-tonality-tells — Shipping unedited AI-generated hero copy with detectable tonality tells (_tokens: 381)
**Mistake**: Publishing AI-generated hero and value-prop copy without editing out the detectable AI tonality tells, on the assumption that AI copy is uniformly fine.

### landing-page-design--showing-fake-or-placeholder-code-in-the-demo-section — Showing fake or placeholder code in the demo section (_tokens: 363)
**Mistake**: Filling the code section with a screenshot of code, pseudo-code, or boilerplate full of foo/bar placeholders and unexplained imports — anything that is not a real, runnable, syntax-highlighted snippet showing actual product usage.

### landing-page-design--showing-mismatched-tier-logos-to-the-wrong-audience — Showing mismatched-tier logos to the wrong audience (_tokens: 272)
**Mistake**: Filling the logo bar with logos that do not fit the target buyer: the world's largest enterprises on a page aimed at SMEs, or unknown small-company logos on a page aimed at enterprises.

### landing-page-design--stacking-every-social-proof-type-into-one-wall-of-proof — Stacking every social proof type into one wall of proof (_tokens: 274)
**Mistake**: Piling logos, testimonials, tweet embeds, badges, and counters all into a single dense block in the hope that more proof is more convincing.

### landing-page-design--stacking-the-comparison-deck-so-you-win-every-row — Stacking the comparison deck so you win every row (_tokens: 459)
**Mistake**: Building a "vs competitor" table where your product wins every single row and the competitor has no green checks anywhere.

### landing-page-design--tap-targets-too-small-or-too-crowded-to-hit-reliably-on-a-phone — Tap targets too small or too crowded to hit reliably on a phone (_tokens: 432)
**Mistake**: Rendering CTA buttons, nav items, or form controls below ~44-48px or packed together with only a few pixels of separation, so a thumb cannot hit the intended target without frequently mis-tapping the neighbor.

### landing-page-design--using-a-heavy-autoplay-hero-video-or-oversized-hero-image-that-wrecks-lcp — Using a heavy autoplay hero video or oversized hero image that wrecks LCP (_tokens: 448)
**Mistake**: Leading the hero with a large autoplay background video, an uncompressed multi-megabyte image, or a hero that only paints after a heavy JavaScript framework hydrates — making the most important element on the page the slowest to appear.

### landing-page-design--using-an-autoplay-video-or-stock-photo-as-the-hero-visual — Using an autoplay video or stock photo as the hero visual (_tokens: 297)
**Mistake**: Filling the hero with an autoplay background video or generic stock photography instead of a real product visual or a concrete claim.

**Mistake**: Filling the code section with a screenshot of code, pseudo-code, or boilerplate full of foo/bar placeholders and unexplained imports — anything that is not a real, runnable, syntax-highlighted snippet showing actual product usage.

### landing-page-design--showing-mismatched-tier-logos-to-the-wrong-audience — Showing mismatched-tier logos to the wrong audience (_tokens: 272)
**Mistake**: Filling the logo bar with logos that do not fit the target buyer: the world's largest enterprises on a page aimed at SMEs, or unknown small-company logos on a page aimed at enterprises.

### landing-page-design--stacking-every-social-proof-type-into-one-wall-of-proof — Stacking every social proof type into one wall of proof (_tokens: 274)
**Mistake**: Piling logos, testimonials, tweet embeds, badges, and counters all into a single dense block in the hope that more proof is more convincing.

### landing-page-design--stacking-the-comparison-deck-so-you-win-every-row — Stacking the comparison deck so you win every row (_tokens: 459)
**Mistake**: Building a "vs competitor" table where your product wins every single row and the competitor has no green checks anywhere.

### landing-page-design--tap-targets-too-small-or-too-crowded-to-hit-reliably-on-a-phone — Tap targets too small or too crowded to hit reliably on a phone (_tokens: 432)
**Mistake**: Rendering CTA buttons, nav items, or form controls below ~44-48px or packed together with only a few pixels of separation, so a thumb cannot hit the intended target without frequently mis-tapping the neighbor.

### landing-page-design--using-a-heavy-autoplay-hero-video-or-oversized-hero-image-that-wrecks-lcp — Using a heavy autoplay hero video or oversized hero image that wrecks LCP (_tokens: 448)
**Mistake**: Leading the hero with a large autoplay background video, an uncompressed multi-megabyte image, or a hero that only paints after a heavy JavaScript framework hydrates — making the most important element on the page the slowest to appear.

### landing-page-design--using-an-autoplay-video-or-stock-photo-as-the-hero-visual — Using an autoplay video or stock photo as the hero visual (_tokens: 297)
**Mistake**: Filling the hero with an autoplay background video or generic stock photography instead of a real product visual or a concrete claim.
